front: Play: Mirabell and Millamant negotiate marriage in the 'proviso scene'; Fainall's blackmail foiled
back: The Way of the World (William Congreve)

front: Play: Valentine feigns madness; Angelica pretends to love Sir Sampson to test Valentine's devotion
Play: source of 'music has charms to soothe a savage breast' and 'hell hath no fury like a woman scorned'
Play: Waitwell pretends to be the rich Sir Rowland to hoodwink Lady Wishfort
back: The Way of the World (William Congreve)

front: Play: opens in a chocolate house; Millamant pins up her hair only with letters in verse, not prose
back: The Way of the World (William Congreve)

front: Play: in the proviso scene, one character demands the other never wear masks at night made from 'hog's bones, hare's gall, pig water, or the marrow of a roasted cat'
back: The Way of the World (William Congreve)

front: Play: prologue spoken by Mr. Betterton contrasts 'natural' fools with 'fools of fortune'
back: The Way of the World (William Congreve)

front: Play: Charles Lamb praised it for interesting the audience in characters 'for whom you care absolutely nothing'
back: The Way of the World (William Congreve)

front: Play: Valentine proclaims himself Truth to avoid signing inheritance to his sailor brother Ben
Play: coined the phrase 'kiss and tell,' used by the gossip Mr. Tattle
Play: Scandal attempts to cuckold the astrology-loving Mr. Foresight
Play: opening scene asks if Plato or Seneca can pay debts; they get creditor Trapland drunk on sack
Play: this author's only tragedy; Zara says 'heav'n has no rage, like love to hatred turn'd'
Play: the nefarious Maskwell schemes against Mellefont's courtship of Cynthia; Brisk cuckolds Lord Froth
Play: Bellmour cuckolds the banker Fondlewife
Author: member of the Kit-Cat Club; friend of Jonathan Swift
Author: wrote a response to Jeremy Collier's 'A Short View of the Immorality and Profaneness of the English Stage'
Author: wrote the libretto for Handel's oratorio Semele, which contains the air 'Where'er you walk'
